ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic sandwich class; no specific falafel sandwich entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers sandwiches and similar products; no falafel-specific code exists. This is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for falafel sandwich; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one falafel sandwich with a side of French fries, weighing approximately 550 g, as listed on the GrabFood menu.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Falafel, homemade (FDC ID 173757) and French fries, deep-fried (FDC ID 170720), composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for falafel (homemade, deep-fried) and French fries (deep-fried), combined with pita bread, fresh vegetables (tomato, lettuce, onion), and plain yogurt sauce in proportions consistent with the described dish. The input per_100g values (energy 154.55 kcal, protein 5.09 g, fat 8.18 g, carbohydrate 15.45 g) are consistent with USDA composite estimates for this dish type and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water data are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and moisture breakdown for this composite dish is not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central for this exact combination.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; falafel in pita bread analogous estimate; chickpea-based foods GI ~28–36, pita bread GI ~57, French fries GI ~75; composite weighted estimate ~57
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a falafel sandwich with French fries as a composite dish. GI estimated as a weighted composite: falafel (chickpea base, GI ~28–36, University of Sydney GI Database), pita bread (GI ~57, University of Sydney GI Database), and French fries deep-fried (GI ~75, University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted by approximate mass proportions in the dish (falafel ~30%, pita ~25%, fries ~30%, vegetables/sauce ~15%), the composite GI is estimated at approximately 57 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 57 × carbohydrate per serving (550 g × 15.45 g/100g = 84.975 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 48.5 g GL. This is a high glycemic load meal.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 218 mg/100g; full serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: At 218 mg sodium per 100 g, a full 550 g serving contributes approximately 1200 mg sodium — over half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — so individuals with hypertension should request unsalted fries, choose a low-sodium yogurt sauce, and limit portion size to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~48.5 per serving) driven by deep-fried French fries (GI ~75) and refined pita bread (GI ~57) with low dietary fibre at 1.82 g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The composite glycemic load of approximately 48.5 per full serving is very high for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; omitting the French fries, substituting wholemeal pita, and increasing the falafel proportion are the most effective modifications to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributes saturated fat (1.45 g/100g) and trans fat (0.09 g/100g); high sodium load at full serving size
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried falafel and French fries contribute saturated and trans fats that raise LDL cholesterol risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request baked or air-fried falafel, avoid the French fries, and monitor total sodium intake from this meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); tomato is a nightshade that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Both the deep-fried falafel and French fries generate AGEs that worsen joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; request baked or air-fried alternatives and monitor individual tolerance to tomato as a nightshade vegetable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Falafel Sandwich with French Fries is a Middle Eastern-inspired fast-food dish sold widely in Singapore through food delivery platforms. The sandwich consists of crispy deep-fried falafel patties — made from ground chickpeas seasoned with herbs and spices — nestled inside a soft bread wrap or pita bread, layered with fresh tomato, sliced onion, and lettuce, and drizzled with a creamy yogurt-based sauce. The meal is accompanied by a side of golden deep-fried French fries. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 155 kcal, 5.1 g protein, 8.2 g fat (including 1.45 g saturated fat and 0.09 g trans fat), and 15.5 g carbohydrates with 1.8 g dietary fibre and 218 mg sodium. The chickpea base of falafel provides plant protein and some fibre, but the deep-fried preparation of both the falafel and the fries significantly raises the fat content and generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 57 (medium), but the glycemic load per full 550 g serving is very high at approximately 48.5, making this a high-glycaemic-load meal. Clinically, this dish is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes without significant modification (omit fries, use wholemeal pita), is of moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and fat content, and is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to deep-fried AGE generation. It is halal-certified and vegetarian but not vegan due to the yogurt sauce. Common allergens include gluten (bread wrap), dairy (yogurt), and potentially sesame (falafel seasoning).
common_user_queries: Is falafel sandwich good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for falafel (homemade, deep-fried) and French fries (deep-fried) as component analogues; the input per_100g values are consistent with these USDA references and are retained. No exact composite dish entry exists in any approved primary source for this specific Singapore GrabFood preparation. GI is an analogous weighted estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite falafel sandwich and fries dish is not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central for this exact combination as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the input source and no approved source provides a direct composite value. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central for this exact preparation. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite dish is not available in the input source and no approved source provides a complete micronutrient panel for this exact Singapore GrabFood preparation as of 2024. All core macros (protein_g 5.09, fat_g 8.18, carbohydrate_g 15.45) are non-zero and sourced from USDA FoodData Central component analogues; no zero-macro justification is required. GI is an analogous weighted composite estimate (falafel chickpea base GI ~28–36, pita bread GI ~57, French fries GI ~75) from University of Sydney GI Database; composite weighted GI ~57 (medium). Glycemic load per serving is very high at ~48.5 for the full 550 g serving. The dish is sold on GrabFood Singapore and is classified as Middle Eastern cuisine adapted for the Singapore market. Input provenance was ai_generated with confidence 0.7; values have been cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central and are consistent.
